entries.prior_belief: The E01 could be mounted directly via ewf.mount_full_image (mmls partition discovery) and then ntfs-3g, per the standard workflow.
entries.new_belief: The E01 is a bare single NTFS volume (no partition table — mmls returns empty), and the acquired ewf1 is exactly 7 sectors (3584 bytes) short of the NTFS-declared volume size, so the backup boot sector tail is missing and ntfs-3g aborts with 'Failed to read last sector (170764286): Invalid argument'. Resolved without modifying evidence by building a device-mapper overlay (linear over the read-only loop + a 7-sector dm-zero tail pad) and mounting that read-only at /mnt/rocba. TSK already reads the volume directly at offset 0, confirming the filesystem is intact.
entries.evidence: fsstat: NTFS 'Windows', Total Sector Range 0-170764286; ewf1 stat size 87431311360 bytes = 170764280 sectors (7 short); ntfs-3g exit 12 'Failed to read last sector'; dmsetup padded device blockdev --getsz = 170764287; mount exit 0.

entries.conclusion:
Analysis of competing hypotheses for SRL-FORGE intrusion and IP exfiltration during 2020-11-13 break-in window.

Key observation: srl-h account predates the break-in (pwd set 2020-10-20, last login 2020-11-10 — the day Fred left). This timing is suspicious but ambiguous: it could be insider pre-positioning, IT provisioning, or attacker preparation through a separate vector.

H1 (HIGH): srl-h is an attacker-controlled local account created during pre-break-in access (insider or remote), used post-theft to exfiltrate via OneDrive Personal sync (7CA57A1EEDBAF486)
- Supporting: unexpected second admin account, password set 2020-10-20 (16 days before Fred's vacation — pre-positioning), last interactive login 2020-11-10 exactly when Fred departed, separate OneDrive Personal GUID bound to this SID, full Administrator rights
- Confirm: Security 4624 Type 2/10 for srl-h with source workstation/IP; OneDrive Personal account email bound in NTUSER\Software\Microsoft\OneDrive\Accounts\Personal for RID 1001; SharePoint/OneDrive file access timestamps post-11-13 under srl-h profile; Jump Lists/Recent items under srl-h pointing to SRL project folders
- Rule out: if 4624 shows only Type 2 from console with no remote source and timing aligns with legitimate IT provisioning ticket

H2 (HIGH): Physical theft + offline access — attacker booted stolen Surface on 2020-11-13+, logged in as srl-h (known local creds), accessed cached OneDrive Business sync roots under fredr profile (already-synced SRL IP on disk), copied via USB
- Supporting: device physically stolen 2020-11-13, USBSTOR/USB/SCSI enum populated in SYSTEM, multiple OneDrive Business sync roots under RID 1002 contain cached IP locally, failed password bursts 2020-11-16 ~00:23-02:50 on Administrator/Guest/DefaultAccount suggest offline/console brute-force attempts
- Confirm: USBSTOR entries with first-insert timestamps 2020-11-13 to 2020-11-19; MountPoints2 under srl-h hive showing post-break-in volumes; LNK/JumpList artifacts referencing removable drive letters; Shellbags under srl-h for Business sync folders; Type 2 console logons post-11-13
- Rule out: no new USB serials post-11-13; no console logons post-theft

H3 (MEDIUM): srl-h is legitimate SRL helpdesk/imaging account from OS upgrade (Windows.old, 2020-11-01), still resident but unused by attacker; actual exfil rode fredr's session via stolen credentials or cookie/token theft, exfiltrating through fredr's OneDrive Business
- Supporting: Windows.old present (upgrade 11-01) ~12 days before srl-h pwd-set anomaly resolves if account pre-dates upgrade; "srl-h" naming suggests SRL helpdesk; fredr last login 2020-11-14 12:51 local is AFTER theft and suggests attacker using Fred's account; bad password 2020-11-14 03:42 suggests credential guessing on fredr
- Confirm: srl-h SID creation time in SAM predating 10-20; no network/file activity under srl-h post-11-10; fredr logon 11-14 from non-Fred source IP; OneDrive Business telemetry showing bulk download/upload under fredr token post-theft
- Rule out: srl-h shows active file access in UserAssist/RecentDocs post-11-13

H4 (LOW-MEDIUM): Insider threat — srl-h is Fred himself or coworker exfiltrating SRL IP pre-vacation, break-in is unrelated/coincidental cover
- Supporting: srl-h activity 11-10 (Fred's last day in office), both accounts admin, separate OneDrive Personal could be Fred's exfil channel
- Confirm: srl-h logon source = console only on dates Fred was present; Personal OneDrive account email = Fred's known personal address; browser history showing personal webmail/cloud uploads
- Rule out: srl-h Personal OneDrive bound to non-Fred email; srl-h active during Fred's documented vacation/travel

H5 (LOW): Failed-password bursts 2020-11-16 represent the actual intrusion attempt (remote), and srl-h/fredr access was legitimate; no successful exfil occurred
- Supporting: brute-force pattern on Administrator/Guest/DefaultAccount classic external RDP attack; RDP-Tcp config present
- Confirm: no 4624 success following 4625 bursts; no anomalous file access; no new OneDrive uploads
- Rule out: any 4624 Type 10 success in window

Exfil channel ranking: (1) OneDrive Personal under srl-h — separate cloud tenant, hardest to detect from SRL side; (2) USB post-theft — offline, leaves USBSTOR traces; (3) OneDrive Business under hijacked fredr — would appear in SRL tenant logs; (4) RDP file copy — needs successful 4624 Type 10.

Decisive discriminators between TOP TWO (H1 remote-controlled srl-h vs H2 physical/offline srl-h post-theft):
- Logon Type for srl-h sessions (Type 2 console = H2; Type 10 RDP / Type 3 network = H1)
- Source workstation/IP in 4624 events
- USB serial first-insert timestamps post-2020-11-13 (H2 signature)
- OneDrive Personal account binding (email) for RID 1001 — identifies controller
- Network egress in memory image (netscan) showing OneDrive Personal endpoints under srl-h processes

OBSERVATION:
CASE_QUESTION: What SRL intellectual property did the actor access or exfiltrate from Fred Rocba's Surface (SRL-FORGE) during the 2020-11-13 home break-in window, and how, where, and when was it moved off the system?

SUPPORTING EVIDENCE:
Host SRL-FORGE, TZ Eastern (EST5EDT, UTC-5). Two interactive local accounts, both Administrators: (1) fredr RID 1002 C:\Users\fredr — the victim Fred, last login 2020-11-14 12:51 local (17:51 UTC), last BAD password 2020-11-14 03:42; (2) srl-h RID 1001 C:\Users\srl-h — UNEXPECTED second account (case brief said single-user), pwd set 2020-10-20, last login 2020-11-10 13:26 local (the day Fred departed on vacation). Failed-password bursts on Administrator/Guest/DefaultAccount on 2020-11-16 ~00:23-02:50. OneDrive Personal sync root for RID 1001 (7CA57A1EEDBAF486) and RID 1002 (E431499DADA298BA); OneDrive Business1 multiple sync roots for RID 1002. SYSTEM hive shows USBSTOR/USB/SCSI enum populated, Terminal Server RDP-Tcp config present. Windows.old present (OS upgrade 2020-11-01). Memory image acquired 2020-11-19. Fred timeline: hired 2020-10-26, departed vacation 2020-11-10, break-in 2020-11-13, device stolen. SRL projects of interest: Metal Alloy Bicycle Helmet, High-Entropy Alloys, Copper Alloy Research, Bulletproof Cotton.

CASE CONTEXT:
External DFIR, single Windows endpoint (Microsoft Surface). Fred is the VICTIM not suspect; his normal pattern is RDP into SRL + cloud apps (O365/SharePoint/OneDrive Personal+Business) using a personal MS account. Any access not matching Fred's vacation absence (2020-11-10 onward) is presumptive intruder activity. Two independent cloud sync paths (Personal + Business OneDrive). Need to disposition BOTH principals (fredr, srl-h) and determine exfil channel (USB, OneDrive Personal, OneDrive Business, email, RDP, web upload). srl-h is a previously-unseen identity whose controller must be bound via an authentication/session artifact (logon type + source) before attributing its actions.

entries.conclusion:
Case analysis follows.

The temporal profile here is critical. The Surface was stolen 2020-11-13, but srl-h (RID 1001) was created 2020-11-01 22:16 — twelve days BEFORE the theft, during the Windows upgrade window. srl-h last logged in 2020-11-10 13:26, the day Fred left for vacation. fredr's last login is 2020-11-14 12:51, AFTER the theft, with a bad-password attempt at 03:42 the same day. This strongly suggests two distinct threat windows: (1) insider/pre-positioned account activity 2020-11-01 through 2020-11-10 under srl-h, and (2) post-theft access under fredr after credential compromise on 2020-11-14. The Administrator/Guest brute-force bursts on 2020-11-16 are a third, likely opportunistic phase by the thief after the targeted exfil already occurred.

Most likely scenarios, ranked:

1. Insider-staged exfiltration: srl-h account created during the 11-01 upgrade as a persistence/cover account, used 11-01 to 11-10 to stage SRL project files to OneDrive Personal (tenant 7CA57A1EEDBAF486) and/or USB while Fred was preparing for vacation. The "break-in" may be cover for the laptop's disappearance after data was already gone.
2. Post-theft targeted access: thief knew fredr's credentials (or reset via srl-h admin), logged in 11-14 12:51, exfiltrated via OneDrive Business (RID1002 has multiple Business roots — high-value SRL tenant data) and/or USB.
3. Opportunistic post-theft brute force 11-16: likely failed lateral attempts, lower priority but must be dispositioned.

The two-principal disposition is mandatory: srl-h must be treated as a distinct actor with its own profile, OneDrive tenant, USB history, browser sessions, and shellbag/RecentDocs trail. Do not conflate.

Highest-yield artifacts first:

- Memory image (acquired 11-19, freshest state): process tree for OneDrive.exe instances per session, explorer.exe per user, any RDP/mstsc, browser processes, command shells. Network connections to OneDrive/Graph/personal storage endpoints. Clipboard and command-line residue.
- SAM + SOFTWARE ProfileList already partially parsed — needs full enumeration for both RIDs and group membership timeline.
- NTUSER.DAT for BOTH fredr AND srl-h: ShellBags (folder access into SRL project paths), RecentDocs, TypedPaths, TypedURLs, UserAssist (program execution per user with timestamps), RunMRU, ComDlg32 OpenSavePidlMRU (exfil save dialogs), Office MRU.
- UsrClass.dat for BOTH users: shellbag continuation, .lnk MRU.
- SOFTWARE SyncRootManager + per-user OneDrive settings under NTUSER (Personal\Accounts\Personal, Business1) to enumerate every synced root path and tenant — both tenants for srl-h AND fredr.
- SYSTEM USBSTOR/USB/SCSI/MountedDevices/MountPoints2 (under each NTUSER) — USB device cross-reference per user session.
- AppCompatCache, Amcache.hve, Prefetch, UserAssist — program execution timeline (archive utilities, rclone, megasync, FTP clients, file shredders).
- Event logs: Security (4624/4625/4634/4648 type 2/3/10), TerminalServices-LocalSessionManager + RemoteConnectionManager, OneDrive operational, USB/PnP (Microsoft-Windows-Partition, Storage-ClassPnP), Application/WLAN.
- Browser artifacts for both profiles: Edge/Chrome history, downloads, logins, cookies — for OneDrive web uploads, webmail (Gmail/Outlook.com Y=, T=, login=, email=, gausr=), file-sharing services.
- $MFT, $UsnJrnl, $LogFile for file lineage of SRL project files (Helmet, HEA, Copper, Cotton).

TTPs to hunt:

- T1136.001 Local Account creation (srl-h on 11-01 during upgrade)
- T1078.003 Valid Accounts: Local
- T1098 Account Manipulation (group membership changes)
- T1052.001 Exfil over USB
- T1567.002 Exfil to Cloud Storage (OneDrive Personal)
- T1048 Exfil over alternative protocol (webmail)
- T1021.001 RDP (fDenyTSConnections setting must be checked — is it 0?)
- T1070.001/.004 Indicator removal (event log clears, file deletion — Windows.old may hide pre-upgrade evidence)
- T1560 Archive Collected Data (7z, rar, zip MRUs)
- T1119 Automated Collection

Tool sequence: memory first (acquired latest, perishable context), then registry identity for both principals in parallel, then disk timeline/filesystem, then events, then browser/network correlation, finishing with cross-artifact MITRE mapping and a srl-h vs fredr cross-reference deliverable.

Red flags that would reprioritize:

- If memory shows active OneDrive.exe with a Business tenant not enumerated in SyncRootManager → pivot immediately to network/OneDrive logs (data still in flight).
- If RDP fDenyTSConnections=0 and 4624 type 10 events exist post-11-13 → pivot to Security log + NLA settings + inbound IPs.
- If srl-h NTUSER shows access to SRL project shellbags BEFORE 11-10 → insider scenario confirmed, deprioritize thief track.
- If $UsnJrnl shows mass file deletion or wevtutil clear → anti-forensics sweep takes priority.
- If amcache shows rclone/megacmd/WinSCP/Cyberduck → cloud exfil tool track jumps ahead of USB.
- If a USB serial appears in srl-h's MountPoints2 but not fredr's (or vice versa) → device attribution per principal becomes the spine of the timeline.

Cross-reference deliverable (mandatory): for each principal (fredr RID1002, srl-h RID1001, Administrator RID500), produce a per-user matrix of logon times, OneDrive tenants, USB serials mounted, SRL project files touched, programs executed, and browser sessions. srl-h must be dispositioned as legitimate-admin vs rogue with explicit evidence.

next_hypothesis_triggers: srl-h NTUSER shellbags reference SRL project folders before 2020-11-10 -> confirm insider exfil track, deprioritize thief track
OneDrive Personal tenant 7CA57A1EEDBAF486 logs show uploads of SRL project files -> pivot to cloud exfil destination attribution
RDP fDenyTSConnections=0 AND Security 4624 type 10 after 2020-11-13 -> pivot to remote access track and external IP enrichment
Amcache or Prefetch shows rclone/megasync/WinSCP/7z/rar execution -> archive+cloud exfil path confirmed
USN journal or Security 1102 shows log clears or mass deletion -> escalate anti-forensics scan ahead of identity work
USB serial in srl-h MountPoints2 distinct from fredr's -> per-principal USB attribution becomes timeline spine
Browser history shows webmail sessions (Gmail/Outlook.com) with attachments of SRL filenames -> add http_session_inventory with login=,email=,user=,gausr=,Y=,T= extraction
fredr 4624 type 2 at 2020-11-14 12:51 with preceding 4625 burst -> credential compromise post-theft confirmed

entries.conclusion:
ANALYSIS

Two RDP identities, two questions: who owns each, and who moved the IP. Let me adversarially split this.

H1 (HIGH): srl-helpdesk@outlook.com = attacker recon/persistence; fred.rocba@outlook.com 11-14 Azure session = same attacker after credential theft; attacker exfilled via OneDrive Personal bound to fred.rocba and/or RDP-redirection to base-rd-08.
- Supports: consumer outlook.com masquerading as helpdesk; srl-h local admin created 2020-11-01 same day as brute-force start (22:15); SDelete staged under srl-h; 260k 4625 flood preceded fredr Type 3 success; 52.249.198.56 is Azure (VPS pivot, not Disney FL residential); Fred physically away with device stolen 11-13; OneDrive Personal already bound to fred.rocba LiveId.
- Refute: residential ISP geolocation of 174.196.200.9 to Fred's home/travel, Fred admitting helpdesk creation, MFA logs on the MS account showing Fred's known devices.

H2 (MEDIUM): srl-helpdesk@outlook.com = legitimate-but-shadow IT (Fred or coworker pre-created backup admin); fred.rocba 11-14 = attacker only.
- Supports: srl-h created 11-01 before brute-force at 22:15 (need exact timestamp ordering); local admin pattern fits sysadmin habits.
- Refute: consumer outlook.com naming convention "srl-helpdesk" is unprofessional for SRL IT; no other SRL helpdesk artifacts; if Fred owned it, expect cross-binding in his profile registry.

H3 (LOW-MEDIUM): Both sessions are attacker, but two DIFFERENT actors (sold access / handoff). 174.196.200.9 residential = initial access broker; 52.249.198.56 Azure = second-stage operator post-theft.
- Supports: 4-day gap, completely different source infrastructure (residential vs cloud), device physically stolen between sessions (11-13).
- Refute: shared TTPs (SDelete, same lateral target), single C2, identical tooling hashes.

H4 (LOW): fred.rocba 11-14 = Fred himself checking in from vacation via Azure VPN/RDP gateway.
- Supports: it IS his personal account, OneDrive Personal binding genuine.
- Refute: device was stolen 11-13 — Fred cannot RDP to a stolen device he's reported missing; Azure consumer IP is not typical Disney hotel WiFi; brute-force context; outbound RDP to internal jump host at 05:00 UTC is operational pivot, not user behavior.

EXFIL CHANNEL RANKING for "which moved SRL IP":
A (HIGH): RDP drive-redirection / interactive copy via base-rd-08 pivot under fredr session (11-14 05:00 + 05:05 outbound RDP, user-initiated logoff = staged pull). Look for tsclient mounts, RDPClip, BitmapCache growth, Jump Lists referencing \\tsclient.
B (MEDIUM): OneDrive Personal sync under fred.rocba (LiveId e431499dada298ba) — check OneDrive Personal logs, SyncDiagnostics, and file telemetry post-11-14 12:31.
C (MEDIUM): USB exfil under srl-h profile (SDelete already present) — USBSTOR under srl-h NTUSER, MountPoints2, setupapi.
D (LOW): OneDrive Business / Gmail — no evidence either is bound; cheap to rule out.

KEY DISCRIMINATORS for H1 vs H2 (top two):
- Creation timestamp ordering of srl-h local account vs 4625 flood start (11-01 22:15) — if srl-h predates flood by hours, H2 rises.
- ProfileList / LiveId binding for srl-helpdesk: is it bound anywhere in registry beyond srl-h NTUSER? Cross-profile presence would suggest pre-existing legit use.
- 174.196.200.9 enrichment (ASN, geo) — residential US South vs hosting.
- Office identity cache and OneDrive account list under both NTUSERs.
- USB serials seen under srl-h vs fredr profiles (different actors leave different sticks).
- Outbound RDP under RID1002 at 05:00 UTC 11-14 — before the 12:31 fredr Type 3 logon — implies a session existed earlier; check 4624 Type 2/10 for fredr before 05:00 and any RunMRU/ShellBags timestamps.

CONCLUSION: Lead = H1 (single attacker, two identities, exfil via base-rd-08 RDP pivot with OneDrive Personal as secondary). Highest-value next steps: parse both NTUSERs for OneDrive/Office account bindings and USB history, dump RDP bitmap cache and tsclient artifacts, enrich both source IPs, and timeline srl-h creation vs brute-force start to the second.

entries.conclusion:
EVIDENCE SUPPORT
- EvtxECmd CSV with 4625 counts per target user is direct support for brute-force volume against multiple usernames.
- Earliest 4625 timestamp 2020-11-01 22:15:31 in Archive-Security-2020-11-02-08-28-14 supports the start time.
- Registry fDenyTSConnections + RDP-Tcp key supports RDP being configured (not necessarily Internet-exposed).

CONTRADICTING / WEAKENING EVIDENCE
- The finding asserts "exposed to the Internet." The supporting evidence cited shows RDP enabled locally in the SYSTEM hive but does NOT include firewall rule state, NAT/edge config, or external source IPs from 4625 IpAddress field. Without LogonType=10 and external IpAddress data from the 4625 records, "Internet-exposed" is inferred, not proven.
- The finding says "RDP/network-pattern dictionary terms" — the actual 4625 LogonType field is not cited. 4625 floods against ADMINISTRATOR are equally consistent with SMB (LogonType 3) brute force, not RDP (LogonType 10).
- "Beginning 2020-11-01 22:15 UTC" — only earliest archive timestamp cited; timezone of EVTX records vs. UTC normalization not stated.

ALTERNATIVE EXPLANATIONS
- SMB/NetBIOS brute force, not RDP. Same 4625 pattern.
- Misconfigured internal service/scanner generating failed logons.
- Password-spray from a compromised internal host post-upgrade.

HALLUCINATION CHECK
- "RDP" attribution is not supported by any cited LogonType field. This is the key overclaim.
- "Internet" exposure not derivable from cited evidence (no external IPs, no firewall artifact cited).
- Username list and counts are concrete and traceable to the CSV — those are fine.
- T1110 (Brute Force) is a valid ATT&CK ID and fits the volumetric pattern regardless of protocol; T1110.001/.003 sub-techniques would need protocol confirmation.

FACT-CHECK
- 260k+ 4625 events is a credible brute-force volume signal.
- The leap from "4625 flood" to "RDP brute force from Internet" requires LogonType 10 + WorkstationName/IpAddress fields, which were not cited.
- Timing correlation with OS upgrade is suggestive but circumstantial.

ADDITIONAL INVESTIGATION
- Parse 4625 LogonType, IpAddress, WorkstationName, AuthenticationPackageName fields to confirm RDP vs SMB and source IP geolocation.
- Check 4624 successes for same accounts post-flood to identify any successful breach.
- Check firewall logs / netscan for TCP/3389 listener and external connections.

VERDICT: CHALLENGED — Brute-force activity (T1110) against the host is well-supported by 4625 volume; the specific claims of RDP protocol and Internet exposure are not derivable from the cited evidence and require 4625 LogonType/IpAddress fields.

next_hypothesis_triggers: if 4625 LogonType=10 dominant then confirm RDP brute force
if 4625 IpAddress shows external/public ranges then confirm Internet exposure
if 4624 success for ADMINISTRATOR after flood then escalate to successful intrusion
if LogonType=3 dominant then re-scope to SMB brute force

entries.conclusion:
EVIDENCE SUPPORT
- 4624 LogonType 10 at 2020-11-10 13:26:11 UTC tied to MicrosoftAccount\srl-helpdesk@outlook.com from 174.196.200.9 — directly supports interactive RDP authentication by that MSA identity.
- Preceding 4624 LogonType 3 NTLM for SRL-FORGE\srl-h at 13:26:09 — supports the local account name involved, but does NOT by itself prove the MSA owns srl-h.
- ProfileList RID 1001 → C:\Users\srl-h — confirms srl-h is RID 1001 with a profile, nothing about MSA linkage.
- SDelete EulaAccepted in srl-h NTUSER — shows SDelete was run under srl-h context; corroborates attacker behavior under that profile but does not bind the MSA.

CONTRADICTING / WEAKENING EVIDENCE
- LogonType 10 with TargetUserName = MicrosoftAccount\srl-helpdesk@outlook.com and a LogonType 3 NTLM for SRL-FORGE\srl-h two seconds prior could be two distinct identity events on the same session negotiation, but they could also represent two different principals (e.g., MSA logon to a different local account, or a network logon from a different host coincident in time). The finding asserts the MSA "controls" srl-h — the binding mechanism is not shown.
- No IdentityProvider/PackageName field cited (Authentication Package, LogonGuid, TargetLinkedLogonId, TargetUserSid) that would tie the MSA logon to RID 1001 (S-1-5-21-...-1001). Without the SID on the 4624 record or a LogonId correlation, the binding is inferred, not proven.
- "consumer outlook.com account masquerading as SRL helpdesk" is a characterization, not evidence. No registry/Cloud AP/IdentityCache artifact cited proving srl-h is linked to the MSA (e.g., HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\IdentityStore, IdentityCache, or LiveId provider entries under the user's NTUSER).

ALTERNATIVE EXPLANATIONS
- The MSA logon may have created/used a different local profile, and the LogonType 3 for srl-h at 13:26:09 may be unrelated (e.g., scheduled task, service enumeration) — temporal adjacency is not causation.
- LogonType 10 with a MicrosoftAccount target is consistent with Windows 10 RDP using MSA sign-in; that does not by itself mean the local account srl-h is the same principal.

HALLUCINATION CHECK
- Claim "MSA controls srl-h" — not directly evidenced. Requires SID match, LinkedLogonId correlation, or IdentityStore registry binding. Currently inferred from name similarity and 2-second proximity.
- "the day Fred departed on vacation" — case context not verified in supporting evidence; treat as case narrative.
- "attacker-operated" — supported circumstantially (SDelete usage under srl-h, off-hours external RDP from a residential-looking IP) but stated as fact.

FACT-CHECK
- T1078 and T1021.001 IDs are valid and applicable.
- LogonType 10 = RemoteInteractive (RDP) — correct.
- LogonType 3 = Network — correct.
- 4624 LogonProcessName "User32" is unusual for a pure RDP MSA logon; typical RDP shows "User32 " or "Advapi  " with Authentication Package = "Negotiate"/"Kerberos"; MSA RDP often shows IdentityProvider. Not contradictory, but worth verifying the full record.
- RID 1001 for a post-install created account is consistent with srl-h created 2020-11-01.

ADDITIONAL INVESTIGATION TO UPGRADE CONFIDENCE

entries.conclusion:
Analysis

1. EVIDENCE SUPPORT
The TerminalServices-RDPClient/Operational log is the canonical source for outbound RDP from a client. EIDs cited:
- 1024: contains the destination hostname the user attempted to connect to (correct).
- 1102: client IP stack info / destination IP resolution (correct semantics — logs the resolved IP for the target).
- 1025: connection established (correct).
- 1026: disconnect reason. The phrase "User-initiated client logoff" is a known disconnect reason string for this provider.
The UserId SID ending in -1002 maps to fredr per case context. Computer=SRL-FORGE matches the stolen Surface. Two sequential session pairs at 05:00:44→05:04:47 and 05:05:36→05:10:44 are internally consistent.

2. CONTRADICTING EVIDENCE
None presented. No corroborating evidence from the destination (base-rd-08 Security 4624 Type 10, TerminalServices-LocalSessionManager 21/22/25) is cited. Without the server-side logs we cannot prove the session actually authenticated successfully — EID 1025 indicates TCP/RDP connection, not necessarily successful interactive logon.

3. ALTERNATIVE EXPLANATIONS
- Fred himself initiated the RDP before the theft window cutoff — but case context says he was on vacation since 11-10 and device stolen 11-13, so 11-14 activity under his account from this host is suspicious.
- Cached credentials / saved RDP file auto-launched by another process. Doesn't change the lateral movement finding, but affects attribution narrative.
- "User-initiated client logoff" (1026) could also fire on certain normal session-end paths; calling it definitively "user-initiated" is accurate to the log string but the actor could have closed the mstsc window — still user-initiated from RDPClient perspective.

4. HALLUCINATION CHECK
- EID 1102 description "Address: 172.16.6.18" — EID 1102 in TerminalServices-RDPClient typically logs "The client has initiated a multi-transport connection to the server <IP>". Field labeling as "Address:" is paraphrased but consistent with the event's content. Acceptable.
- EID 1024 message field is destination string; consistent.
- EID 1025: "connected" is paraphrase; actual message is "RDP ClientActiveX has been disconnected (Reason= 0)" or "The client has established a connection"  depending on provider. Minor paraphrase risk but not a fabrication.
- EID 1026: "User-initiated client logoff" — this is a documented disconnect reason code from the RDPClient provider. Plausible.
- Times, SIDs, hostnames are specific and traceable to the cited CSV. No invented offsets.

5. FACT-CHECK
- T1021.001 (Remote Services: Remote Desktop Protocol) — correct technique ID and accurate mapping.
- SID structure S-1-5-21-<domain>-1002 with RID 1002 — valid Windows SID format.
- 172.16.6.18 in RFC1918 internal range — consistent with internal AD.
- Claim says "two short sessions each ending in user-initiated client logoff" — supported by two 1026 events. Fine.
- The finding states the actor performed this. Attribution to "the actor" (vs. Fred) rests on case context (vacation + theft) not on the log itself. This is reasonable inference but should be flagged as inference, not log fact.

6. ADDITIONAL INVESTIGATION
- Pull base-rd-08 Security.evtx for 4624 Type 10 / 4625 at 05:00:44–05:10:44 UTC for fredr → confirms authentication outcome.
- TerminalServices-LocalSessionManager/Operational and RemoteConnectionManager on base-rd-08 (EIDs 21, 22, 25, 41, 23).
- SRL-FORGE Security.evtx for 4648 (explicit credential use) around 05:00:43 — identifies if cached or typed creds.
- Prefetch/Amcache for mstsc.exe on SRL-FORGE near 05:00:44.
- Default.rdp / RDP MRU under fredr NTUSER.DAT to check if 172.16.6.18 / base-rd-08 was pre-saved.

7. VERDICT: SUPPORTED
RDP connection attempts from SRL-FORGE to base-rd-08 under fredr's SID at the stated times are well-supported by the cited RDPClient operational events. Authentication success and "actor vs. Fred" attribution are inferences requiring server-side corroboration.

entries.conclusion:
EVIDENCE SUPPORT
The finding cites evtx_security_logons_all.csv with two 4624 events on 2020-11-14 at 12:31:26 and 12:52:03 UTC, LogonType 10, target principal MicrosoftAccount\fred.rocba@outlook.com, LogonProcessName User32, RemoteHost field showing SRL-FORGE (52.249.198.56). The MSA-to-fredr-profile binding is sourced from call 65 (Office identity e431499dada298ba_LiveId / OneDrive Personal). These are concrete, named field values, which is good.

CONTRADICTING EVIDENCE / WEAKNESSES
- LogonProcessName User32 with LogonType 10 is unusual. Typical interactive RDP 4624s show LogonProcessName User32 only when the session is established via winlogon at the console transition; pure network RDP usually shows LogonProcessName as Advapi or similar with AuthenticationPackageName Negotiate/Kerberos. User32 + Type 10 can appear, but the combination warrants the raw event text, not a summarized CSV row.
- The RemoteHost field 'SRL-FORGE (52.249.198.56)' embeds a workstation name. SRL-FORGE could be the local host name being logged, not the remote source. The IpAddress field is the authoritative remote source — that specific field value is not quoted in the supporting evidence. This is a meaningful gap.
- "52.249.198.56 is in Microsoft Azure space" — asserted without a cited WHOIS/RDAP lookup. The /8 belongs to Microsoft, but specific allocation (Azure vs. Microsoft corporate vs. O365 front-end) needs an enrichment call.
- Two 4624s 20 minutes apart with the same MSA from the same IP is consistent with attacker activity, but also consistent with legitimate MSA token refresh / reconnect from a Microsoft service edge (e.g., RD Gateway, Azure Virtual Desktop, or Microsoft account sync). MSA logons of LogonType 10 from a Microsoft-owned IP could be a Microsoft service relay rather than an attacker pivot.

ALTERNATIVE EXPLANATIONS
- Legitimate Azure Virtual Desktop / Windows 365 / RD Gateway session: if Fred's tenant uses any Microsoft cloud RDP brokering, the source IP on the on-prem 4624 can legitimately be an Azure egress IP. This needs to be ruled out before calling it attacker activity.
- Fred enabled Remote Desktop via MSA (Allow connections from computers running any version) and connected from a hotel/airport WiFi that egresses through a Microsoft service. Unlikely but possible.
- The session is a continuation/refresh of an earlier compromise — the attacker, not Fred personally, used a previously stolen MSA credential. The finding actually allows this, but the wording "attacker use of Fred's personal account" is the correct framing.
- SRL-FORGE may be the victim host's own name being logged in WorkstationName field; the 52.249.198.56 is in the IpAddress field. This is the most likely reading but it is not explicitly confirmed in the cited evidence.

HALLUCINATION CHECK
- "Microsoft Azure IP" — stated as fact, no WHOIS/RDAP/enrich.* citation. Flag as unverified specificity.
- "cloud relay" — interpretive conclusion, not a tool finding. Stylistic, acceptable in a hypothesis but not as fact.
- "device stolen 2020-11-13, Fred on vacation in Florida" — case context, not in the cited tool output. Assumed from case background.
- The specific 4624 field decomposition (which field carried 52.249.198.56 vs. SRL-FORGE) is not explicitly broken out in the excerpt; this matters.

FACT-CHECK
- 4624 LogonType 10 = RemoteInteractive (RDP/Terminal Services). Correct.
- MicrosoftAccount\ prefix on TargetUserName for MSA logon is a real Windows behavior on Win10+ when MSA is linked. Correct.
- ATT&CK mapping (not claimed explicitly): T1078.003 Valid Accounts: Local Accounts or T1078.004 Cloud Accounts; T1021.001 Remote Services: RDP — all valid IDs.
- Negative finding scrutiny: not applicable, this is a positive identification finding.

ADDITIONAL INVESTIGATION
- Pull the raw 4624 XML for both events to confirm IpAddress field = 52.249.198.56 and WorkstationName field separately. Use misc.evtx_dump or misc.evtx_filter on Security.evtx for EventRecordIDs around those timestamps.
- Run enrich.* (or document via WHOIS) to confirm 52.249.198.56 ASN/allocation is Azure customer space vs. Microsoft service edge. If it's a known AVD/RDGW prefix the finding weakens significantly.
- Correlate with 4778/4779 (session reconnect) and TerminalServices-LocalSessionManager Operational log for session IDs at 12:31 and 12:52 to confirm interactive shell activity.
- Check 4648 explicit credential events around those times for the source-side process.
- Pull RDP bitmap cache / ConnectionGraph if disk image available.

VERDICT: UNCERTAIN — the two 4624 MSA Type-10 events are real and well-cited, but "Azure IP" is unverified by enrichment, the IpAddress vs. WorkstationName field separation is not explicit in the excerpt, and benign Microsoft service-edge explanations have not been excluded.

next_hypothesis_triggers: If 52.249.198.56 resolves to a Microsoft service-edge prefix (AVD/RDGW), downgrade attacker-pivot claim
If raw 4624 XML shows IpAddress=52.249.198.56 and WorkstationName=SRL-FORGE separately, upgrade source-IP confidence
Correlate 4624 with 4778/4779 and TSLSM session IDs to confirm interactive shell vs token-only logon
Check 4648 explicit-cred events to see if MSA was used as a credential pivot from the earlier fredr Type 3 sessions

entries.conclusion:
Analysis

1. EVIDENCE SUPPORT
- SAM InternetName binding: RegRipper samparse output explicitly ties RID 1001 (srl-h) to InternetName srl-helpdesk@outlook.com. This is the strongest single artifact — InternetName is the documented SAM field MS populates when a local account is linked to a Microsoft consumer account (cached MSA logon). This is a true principal binding, not temporal correlation.
- Administrators membership: call 36 cited as evidence srl-h is in Administrators group.
- Interactive RDP: 4624 LogonType 10 with TargetUserName MicrosoftAccount\srl-helpdesk@outlook.com at 13:26:11 from 174.196.200.9 — directly supports remote interactive logon by the MSA-bound principal.
- Paired Type 3 at 13:26:09 for SRL-FORGE\srl-h aligns with normal RDP network-then-interactive sequence.
- Last Login timestamp in SAM (13:26:09) matches the Type 3 event — internally consistent.

2. CONTRADICTING EVIDENCE
- None presented. Type 3 username appears as SRL-FORGE\srl-h (local) while Type 10 appears as MicrosoftAccount\srl-helpdesk — this is expected MSA behavior (network auth resolves local SAM, interactive presents MSA identity) and not a contradiction, but worth noting.

3. ALTERNATIVE EXPLANATIONS
- Legitimate admin who linked their personal MS account for convenience (still policy violation, not necessarily attacker). Case context (single-user Surface, srl-h not expected, consumer domain outside corp tenant) weakens this but does not eliminate it.
- 174.196.200.9 attribution to "external" assumes non-corporate IP — not verified in evidence (no WHOIS/ASN cited). The finding calls it external but supporting evidence does not document the geolocation/ownership lookup.

4. HALLUCINATION CHECK
- "external source 174.196.200.9" — IP is in evidence; "external" characterization is reasonable (public IP space, not RFC1918) but no WHOIS lookup cited. Minor.
- T1078 and T1021.001 are valid MITRE IDs and correctly describe the behaviors (Valid Accounts; Remote Services: RDP).
- SDelete EulaAccepted in srl-h NTUSER is cited but is not used to support the binding claim itself — it's contextual. Not a hallucination, but note SDelete EulaAccepted only proves the EULA dialog was dismissed once under that profile, not that SDelete was executed maliciously.
- No invented offsets, no fabricated mechanism. The InternetName→MSA mapping is a real documented SAM artifact.

5. FACT-CHECK
- SAM InternetName field: legitimate, populated by Windows when local account is linked to MSA. ✓
- LogonType 10 = RemoteInteractive (RDP/Terminal Services). ✓
- MicrosoftAccount\ prefix in TargetUserName for MSA logons. ✓
- T1078 (Valid Accounts) and T1021.001 (Remote Services: RDP) — both valid and correctly applied. ✓
- Timestamps internally consistent (SAM Last Login matches Type 3, Type 10 follows 2s later — normal RDP sequence). ✓

6. ADDITIONAL INVESTIGATION
- Source IP enrichment: WHOIS/geolocation on 174.196.200.9 to confirm non-corporate origin.
- Check for prior 4624 events for srl-h to establish whether 2020-11-10 13:26 is first-ever interactive logon or a known recurring pattern.
- Check Microsoft-Windows-TerminalServices-LocalSessionManager/Operational (EID 21, 25) for session reconnect history.
- Check NetworkList registry for the 174.196.200.9 connection profile.
- Correlate srl-h profile creation time (ProfileList) vs account creation in SAM vs first 4624 — establishes whether account was provisioned shortly before use (anomaly) or long-standing.
- Pull subsequent process activity under srl-h SID to attribute post-logon actions.

7. VERDICT: SUPPORTED — InternetName field is an authoritative principal binding, not temporal inference; 4624 Type 10 with MicrosoftAccount\ prefix corroborates the same identity; timestamps align. Attacker-vs-insider attribution remains open but the identity binding itself is solid.

entries.conclusion:
EVIDENCE SUPPORT
- 548,244 Security 4625 events with LogonType 3 tally and target-username distribution come directly from evtx_security_logons_all.csv parse (call 67). Concrete.
- External source IPs enumerated with hit counts; none in RFC1918 — supports "from Internet".
- Earliest timestamp 2020-11-01 22:15:31 sourced from the same parse, archive file named Archive-Security-2020-11-02-08-28-14.
- SYSTEM hive RDP enabled config (call 34) supports remote access exposure.

CONTRADICTING / WEAKENING EVIDENCE
- LogonType 3 = Network logon (SMB/NTLM/WinRM/etc.), NOT RDP. RDP brute force is normally LogonType 10 (or 3 with NLA in some cases, but the negotiated auth ends up as 10 on success). The finding's headline says "RDP/remote access reachable from the Internet" — RDP exposure is asserted from the registry, but the 548k floods are LogonType 3, which is SMB/NTLM over 445 or similar. So the brute force is most likely SMB/NTLM, not RDP. The finding partially self-corrects in CASE CONTEXT but the headline still couples RDP with the flood, which is misleading.
- No evidence cited that port 445 (or any specific service) was actually internet-reachable. The registry only proves RDP (3389) configured to accept connections, not that SMB was exposed.
- "Right after the OS upgrade" — no evidence cited for the OS upgrade timestamp; this is a correlation claim without a cited artifact.

ALTERNATIVE EXPLANATIONS
- LogonType 3 floods could be NTLM relay attempts, scanner noise, or authenticated SMB scanning rather than a deliberate targeted brute force.
- Some "external" IPs may be misattributed if the host is behind NAT or has VPN/tunneling — but the IP list looks like commodity scanner sources.
- The flood could predate the upgrade and only be visible in retained logs because older logs rolled — earliest-observed ≠ earliest-occurred.

HALLUCINATION CHECK
- "Right after the OS upgrade" — upgrade time not in supporting evidence. Flag.
- "NTLM network-logon" — LogonType 3 supports network logon; NTLM specifically is not proven (could be Kerberos, though brute force against local Administrator strongly implies NTLM). Reasonable inference but not directly evidenced; authentication package field not cited.
- "RDP… reachable from the Internet" — registry shows RDP enabled, not internet-reachable. No firewall/netstat/pcap cited.

FACT-CHECK
- T1110 Brute Force — correct ATT&CK ID, correct technique.
- LogonType 3 = Network logon — correct.
- LogonType 10 = RemoteInteractive (RDP) — correct, matches CASE CONTEXT note.
- 4625 = failed logon — correct.
- Volume + target-name pattern (Administrator/admin/test/user) is canonical brute-force signature — supported.
- Headline conflates RDP exposure with the LogonType 3 flood. The flood is almost certainly SMB/NTLM (445) or another network-auth service, not RDP.

ADDITIONAL INVESTIGATION
- Parse the AuthenticationPackageName and LmPackageName fields from 4625 to confirm NTLM vs Kerberos.
- Identify which service was targeted: check 4625 ProcessName/WorkstationName; check firewall logs or netstat-from-memory for listening 445.
- Confirm OS upgrade timestamp via Setup event log / registry CurrentVersion InstallDate before asserting temporal correlation.
- Check whether any of these LogonType 3 attempts succeeded (4624 LogonType 3 from same IPs).

VERDICT: CHALLENGED — The brute-force activity itself (548k 4625, LogonType 3, external IPs, common admin usernames) is well-supported. But the headline incorrectly characterizes it as targeting RDP; LogonType 3 is network logon (SMB/NTLM), not RDP. The "right after OS upgrade" temporal claim lacks a cited upgrade timestamp.

next_hypothesis_triggers: If AuthenticationPackageName=NTLM in 4625, confirm NTLM brute force; if Kerberos, revise
If WorkstationName/ProcessName in 4625 indicates SMB, restate headline as SMB/NTLM brute force not RDP
If any 4624 LogonType 3 from the same source IPs exists, escalate to successful network-logon compromise
If Setup event log or InstallDate registry confirms an OS upgrade immediately before 2020-11-01 22:15, retain the temporal correlation claim; otherwise drop it

entries.conclusion:
EVIDENCE SUPPORT
The four cited EIDs (1024, 1102, 1025, 1026) from TerminalServices-RDPClient/Operational are the canonical client-side RDP outbound trail:
- 1024 = RDP ClientActiveX is trying to connect to server (hostname)
- 1102 = client initiated multi-transport connection to server (IP)
- 1025 = client connected
- 1026 = client disconnected (with reason code; "user-initiated" depends on reason field)
Two sequences at 05:00:44–05:04:47 and 05:05:36–05:10:44 are consistent with two distinct outbound sessions to the same target. UserId SID ending -1002 maps to Fred's RID per case context.

CONTRADICTING EVIDENCE
None cited in the finding. No EID 1026 reason code is shown verbatim — "user-initiated logoff" is the analyst's interpretation, not a quoted field value.

ALTERNATIVE EXPLANATIONS
- Pre-theft scheduled/cached activity is unlikely given the timestamps, but the device-theft timing (2020-11-13) and post-theft RDP under Fred's SID is consistent with the working theory of stolen-device misuse. The activity itself is a normal RDP client connection; only the case context (Fred on vacation, device stolen) makes it suspicious.
- Could also be legitimate remote management by IT staff using Fred's cached credentials, or Fred himself remoting in during vacation. The finding appropriately scopes to "client-side connection confirmed" without overclaiming auth success.

HALLUCINATION CHECK
- "User-initiated client logoff" for EID 1026: EID 1026 is "The client has disconnected from the server" — the disconnect reason is encoded in a Reason field (e.g., reason code 1, 2, 3...). The finding asserts "user-initiated" but does not quote the reason code/field. This is a plausible but unverified characterization.
- T1021.001 (Remote Desktop Protocol) is a valid ATT&CK sub-technique and correctly applied to outbound RDP initiation.
- SID structure S-1-5-21-...-1002 correctly parses to a domain/local RID 1002 user.
- The finding properly caveats "destination-side auth outcome not in evidence" — good discipline.

FACT-CHECK
- EID-to-meaning mapping is accurate per Microsoft documentation for Microsoft-Windows-TerminalServices-ClientActiveXCore/Microsoft-Windows-TerminalServices-RDPClient/Operational.
- IP 172.16.6.18 is in RFC1918 space, consistent with "internal SRL host."
- Two sessions, ~4 min and ~5 min duration, are plausible jump-host interactive sessions.
- Claim is appropriately scoped (client-side only).

ADDITIONAL INVESTIGATION
- Quote the actual EID 1026 reason code from raw evtx to substantiate "user-initiated" (vs. network drop, server-initiated, etc.). Run misc.evtx_dump or ez.evtxecmd on the source evtx and capture the EventData Reason field.
- Correlate with Security.evtx 4648 (explicit credential use) and 4624 type 3/10 on SRL-FORGE around 05:00:44 to confirm the auth context (which credentials were presented).
- If base-rd-08 logs ever become available, pull 4624 type 10 / TerminalServices-LocalSessionManager 21/22/25 for fredr at those timestamps.
- Check SRL-FORGE Default.rdp / RDP bitmap cache and Registry MRU (Software\Microsoft\Terminal Server Client\Servers) for base-rd-08 entry.

VERDICT: SUPPORTED — client-side outbound RDP to 172.16.6.18 under fredr's SID is well-supported by the cited EID chain; the "user-initiated logoff" qualifier is minor overreach pending the EID 1026 reason field, and the finding correctly refrains from claiming destination auth success.

next_hypothesis_triggers: Quote EID 1026 Reason code to substantiate user-initiated disconnect
Correlate Security 4648/4624 on SRL-FORGE at 05:00:44 and 05:05:37 to confirm credential context
Enumerate Terminal Server Client MRU and bitmap cache for base-rd-08 to corroborate first-use vs habitual target

entries.conclusion:
1. EVIDENCE SUPPORT
- USB device identity: RegRipper usbstor output naming "Lexar USB Flash Drive" with S/N AAZ62W7KENRSJLHY, with Last Removal timestamp 2020-11-14 04:34:56 UTC. Directly supports device identification and removal time.
- Drive letter mapping: RegRipper mountdev mapping USBSTOR Lexar AAZ62W7KENRSJLHY -> \DosDevices\F:. Supports F: assignment.
- File targets and timestamps: LECmd output of LNK files in fredr's Recent folder showing VolumeLabel CRIMSON2, VolSN CA659866, TargetCreated timestamps 2020-11-14 03:45-04:30 UTC pointing to F:\... SRL project paths. Supports that these files existed on F: at those times and were accessed/opened from fredr's account.

2. CONTRADICTING EVIDENCE
- None presented. However, LNK TargetCreated reflects the creation time of the target file as recorded when the shortcut was generated — this typically occurs on file access/open, not strictly on write. The finding equates TargetCreated on a removable volume with "written to (bytes transferred to) the USB," which is an inference, not a direct write log. USN/$MFT on the USB itself would be needed to confirm copy-to operations.

3. ALTERNATIVE EXPLANATIONS
- The files may have already existed on the USB (e.g., staged earlier) and were merely opened during the 03:45-04:30 window, generating LNKs. TargetCreated would then reflect prior creation on F:, not exfil at that moment — though TargetCreated dates of 2020-11-14 on a device first installed 2020-11-10 still imply recent placement.
- The fredr session could be a legitimate remote/console session by Fred or by a sysadmin using fredr's credentials; attribution to "theft" needs the logon-source corroboration (already referenced as Type 3 from where?).
- "Files from SRL system" and "Files of interest\Recovered Documents" path names suggest the operator may have been collecting/staging from other sources to USB — consistent with exfil but path naming alone is suggestive, not probative.

4. HALLUCINATION CHECK
- All timestamps, serials, volume labels, and paths trace to cited RegRipper/LECmd outputs. No invented offsets.
- The claim "files written to (bytes transferred to) the USB" is an interpretive leap from LNK TargetCreated — not a direct write/copy event source. This is a mechanism claim that should be softened or corroborated with $MFT/USN from the USB or Shellbags/jumplists showing copy actions.
- "Exfiltration" as intent is inferred from context (post-theft, fredr on vacation); evidence supports data movement to removable media, not necessarily transport off-prem — though T1052.001 covers USB write regardless.

5. FACT-CHECK
- T1052.001 (Exfiltration Over USB) is a valid MITRE sub-technique and correctly applied to data copied to USB media.
- usbstor First Install and Last Removal fields are legitimate RegRipper outputs from SYSTEM hive USBSTOR/MountedDevices keys.
- LECmd VolumeLabel/VolumeSerialNumber/TargetCreated are real LNK fields.
- Two different VolSNs would normally be a concern, but CA659866 is the FAT/NTFS volume serial (4 bytes) and AAZ62W7KENRSJLHY is the USB hardware serial — these are different artifacts and consistent.
- Last Removal 04:34:56 post-dating last file activity 04:30:20 is internally consistent.

6. ADDITIONAL INVESTIGATION
- ez.jlecmd on fredr's AutomaticDestinations/CustomDestinations to confirm open vs. save-to actions and corroborate timeline.
- ez.recmd_hive on NTUSER for fredr Shellbags to show F:\ folder navigation by fredr.
- ez.mftecmd / USN journal from the host showing source file READs of SRL paths immediately preceding LNK TargetCreated times (correlate.process_to_file).
- ez.evtxecmd for Security 4624/4634 to confirm fredr Type 3 logon source IP and session duration; Microsoft-Windows-Partition/Diagnostic and Storage/Operational for USB insert/remove confirmation.
- ez.appcompatcacheparser / ez.amcacheparser to confirm no copy utility (robocopy/xcopy) execution context.
- If the USB itself is in evidence: tsk.fls/ez.mftecmd on F: to get authoritative file creation/copy timestamps.

7. VERDICT: SUPPORTED (with one caveat) — Device identity, drive letter, volume label, file paths, and timeline are well supported by independent registry and LNK artifacts. The specific mechanism "bytes transferred to USB" is inferred from LNK TargetCreated on removable media and should ideally be corroborated by $MFT/USN from the USB or host-side copy evidence; the inference is reasonable but not direct.

next_hypothesis_triggers: If Security 4624 shows fredr Type 3 source IP external or from compromised host, escalate to confirmed insider/account-takeover exfil
If Shellbags show fredr navigating SRL source folders prior to 03:45, supports staging-then-copy workflow
If USN/$MFT shows source-file reads immediately before TargetCreated timestamps, upgrades 'written to USB' from inference to direct evidence
If no copy utility execution found in Amcache/AppCompatCache, indicates manual Explorer copy

entries.conclusion:
Final logic and confidence check follows.

LOGICAL GAPS
- G1. Brute-force (Finding 2) → successful logon link is not established. 548K 4625s but no 4624 Type3 success from any of those external IPs is cited. The chain from brute force to the srl-h RDP (Finding 1, Type10 from Verizon) is not evidenced — these may be unrelated activity streams.
- G2. Initial access for fredr account compromise is not explained. Finding 4 asserts "post-theft access" from Azure but no artifact shows credential capture, phishing, or password change preceding the 2020-11-14 12:31 logon.
- G3. SDelete execution (Finding 9) → what was deleted is not enumerated. Prefetch proves execution but USN/$LogFile/MFT analysis for deletion targets is not cited.
- G4. Google Drive staging (Finding 6) → actual upload/sync to cloud is not evidenced. Files in G:\My Drive local cache ≠ confirmed upload; Drive File Stream logs / sync database (DriveFS metadata) not cited.
- G5. backup.pst origin (Finding 7) — was it produced by Outlook export, scanpst, or third-party? No tool execution artifact cited.

CONTRADICTIONS
- None material. Finding 10 properly excludes the IRT drive.

TIER VIOLATIONS
- Finding 1 [CONFIRMED]: SAM InternetName binding + 4624 Type10 + source IP — physical artifacts with corroboration. Tier OK.
- Finding 3 [CONFIRMED]: outbound RDP to internal host — needs cited 4648/TerminalServices-Client artifact on SRL-FORGE. If only inferred from destination-side logs (not in scope here, single endpoint), this should be LIKELY. Verify source-side artifact exists; otherwise downgrade to LIKELY.
- Finding 5 [CONFIRMED]: USBSTOR + MountedDevices + LNK + volume label is sufficient for CONFIRMED for device attachment. "Files placed/removed" timeline depends on LNK MAC + $USN; if only LNK target timestamps cited (no USN), the file-movement portion is LIKELY, not CONFIRMED. Split tier.
- Finding 6 [SUSPECTED] for cloud channel claim is appropriate given no upload evidence. Tier OK.
- Finding 9 [LIKELY]: SDelete download + prefetch = execution is CONFIRMED tier for execution itself (prefetch is a physical artifact). The anti-forensics *purpose* (T1070.004 — deletion of evidence) remains LIKELY/SUSPECTED until deletion targets are identified. Split: execution CONFIRMED, anti-forensic intent LIKELY.

TIER CONTRADICTIONS
- None. Findings 1 and 4 both describe outlook.com→SAM binding + RDP but for different principals at different tiers, justified by different evidence completeness (srl-h has direct Verizon residential IP correlation; fredr Azure IP is more ambiguous).

OVERCLAIMED MECHANISMS
- O1. Finding 2 maps to T1110 (Brute Force) — supported. But proximity in narrative to Finding 1 implies causation; no evidence the brute force succeeded or related to srl-h access.
- O2. Finding 6 T1567.002 (Exfil to Cloud Storage) — local staging in DriveFS cache folder is not proof of cloud exfiltration. Either downgrade MITRE mapping to T1074 (Local Staging) until upload evidence found, or keep T1567.002 with SUSPECTED qualifier explicit.
- O3. Finding 9 — "EULA accepted under fredr+srl-h" implies both users ran SDelete; need separate NTUSER EULA registry key cites per principal.

MISSING INVESTIGATION / EVIDENCE EXHAUSTION
- M1. BLOCKER: Memory image (19GB) netscan timed out and was deferred. Process listing (psscan/pslist), cmdline, and network artifacts from memory have NOT been exhausted. Memory is in-scope evidence and could resolve G3 (SDelete deletion targets via handles), G4 (DriveFS process state, upload connections), and G1 (active sessions at time of capture). Cannot finalize report claiming disk is "authoritative" without at least psscan + netscan retry with smaller scan window or pslist+cmdline as fallback.
- M2. BLOCKER: Security 4624 Type3 successes from the 4625 source IP set were not cross-referenced. Without this, Finding 2 cannot be tied to or excluded from the access chain. Filter Security.evtx archives for 4624 Type3 where IpAddress ∈ {external 4625 sources}.
- M3. ADVISORY: DriveFS metadata (sync_log, metadata_sqlite_db under %LOCALAPPDATA%\Google\DriveFS\) not parsed — would upgrade Finding 6 from SUSPECTED to LIKELY/CONFIRMED.
- M4. ADVISORY: USN Journal / $LogFile for 2020-11-14 13:37-14:30 window not cited — would identify SDelete targets and confirm staged-file lifecycle.
- M5. ADVISORY: Amcache + AppCompatCache for sdelete.exe, outlook.exe export action, any archiver (7z/winrar) — not cited in coverage.
- M6. ADVISORY: fredr NTUSER\...\TypedURLs, TypedPaths, RecentDocs, Shellbags around 2020-11-14 03:45-14:00 to corroborate manual file selection.
- M7. ADVISORY: Browser history (Edge/Chrome) for sdelete.zip download referrer and any webmail / Drive web UI use — not cited.
- M8. ADVISORY: Suspect-list cross-reference. Case context lists two principals (srl-h, fredr) bound to outlook.com identities; were these names cross-referenced against an SRL HR/employee roster or known-suspect list to confirm fredr = legitimate employee account (insider) vs. attacker-created? Not stated.

AUDIT_FINDINGS:
[]

READY_TO_REPORT: false
BLOCKING_ISSUES (1): Latest reason.synthesize still labels one or more gaps as BLOCKER. Return to Triage/Collect/Analyze as needed, run the missing evidence work, then re-run reason.synthesize before Report. Do not try to satisfy this by rewording findings.
WARNINGS (1): Findings span 3 hosts (172.16.6.18, 174.196.200.9, 52.249.198.56) but no correlate.process_to_file or correlate.network_to_process call was made. Call them (with no PID/IP/path filter) before reason.synthesize so the timeline reflects cross-host joins, not isolated per-host slices.

Final logic and confidence check follows.

LOGICAL GAPS

G1. Finding 6 (Google Drive exfil) — no evidence cited that files in G:\My Drive\STARK-RESEARCH-LABS FOLDER actually completed upload to Google's cloud. DriveFS sync_log / cloud-side confirmation absent. Local staging is evidenced; cloud egress is inferred. SUSPECTED tier is correct.

G2. Finding 7 — backup.pst creation at 13:39 UTC and SRL-EMAIL-EXPORT.pst at 14:00:54 UTC are filesystem-evidenced, but the causal link between the two (i.e. that SRL-EMAIL-EXPORT.pst is a copy/rename of backup.pst, or that both derive from the same Outlook export operation) is not stated. Hash comparison or size correlation would tighten this.

G3. Finding 9 — SDELETE.EXE prefetch confirms execution but does not evidence WHAT was deleted. USN journal gap analysis (af.af_usn_gaps) is referenced as residual but would establish the deletion targets and tie SDelete to specific anti-forensic intent on SRL artifacts.

G4. Finding 2 — 548,244 4625 events from 332 IPs is established, but no finding documents whether any of the targeted accounts (ADMINISTRATOR/ADMIN/USER/FORGE/TEST) ever produced a 4624 success. Finding 11 only addresses fredr/fred.rocba. A non-finding ("no success on enumerated-target accounts") should be explicit.

CONTRADICTIONS

None identified. Finding 10 properly excludes the IR acquisition drive from exfil attribution.

TIER VIOLATIONS

None. Finding 6 is correctly SUSPECTED (cloud egress not confirmed). Finding 9 is correctly LIKELY (execution evidenced, scope of deletion not). Finding 11 is appropriately LIKELY rather than CONFIRMED given it rests on logon-source correlation rather than a single artifact tying brute-force success to the takeover session.

TIER CONTRADICTIONS

None. The fredr/fred.rocba account-takeover mechanism is consistently LIKELY across Findings 4 and 11; the RDP-inbound mechanism is CONFIRMED at the event-log layer (Finding 3) and LIKELY at the MSA-binding layer (Finding 4) — these are different mechanistic claims (outbound internal RDP vs. inbound MSA-authenticated RDP), so no contradiction.

OVERCLAIMED MECHANISMS

O1. Finding 1 states srl-h was "provisioned during imaging (pwd 2020-10-20)" — the pwd-last-set date is evidenced but the inference that this represents imaging-time provisioning (rather than a later password change) should be supported by SAM creation timestamp or initial logon event, or downgraded to interpretive context.

O2. Finding 6 phrase "exported mailbox staged into G:\My Drive" — the staging into G: is filesystem-evidenced; the characterization as a Google Drive "channel" (i.e. exfiltration mechanism) presumes sync occurred. Acceptable at SUSPECTED tier but the report should not narrate this as completed exfiltration.

MISSING INVESTIGATION

M1. Suspect-roster cross-reference: case context references SRL personnel (Maria Hill, Timothy Dungan) and an SRL employee/insider question is implicit in the fredr account compromise. The roster cross-ref against srl-helpdesk@outlook.com and fred.rocba@outlook.com MSA identities is listed as residual advisory. Per the evidence-exhaustion rule: if a suspect roster was available in case context and the two bound MSA identities were never compared against it, this is a BLOCKER. Coverage statement calls it advisory — confirm whether a roster exists in case context. If yes, BLOCKER; if no roster was ever provided, advisory stands.

M2. DriveFS sync_log (%LOCALAPPDATA%\Google\DriveFS\Logs\sync_log.log) — would convert Finding 6 from SUSPECTED to LIKELY/CONFIRMED. Listed as advisory; acceptable since Finding 6 is already correctly tiered SUSPECTED.

M3. USN journal gap analysis for SDelete deletion targets (af.af_usn_gaps) — would tie Finding 9 to specific files. Advisory.

M4. Explicit non-finding on brute-force target accounts (per G4) — should be stated in report.

BLOCKERS vs ADVISORIES

The suspect-roster cross-reference (M1) is the only candidate blocker and is conditional on whether a roster was provided in case context. The coverage statement asserts it is a "documented limitation" — this implies the team has decided no roster cross-ref is feasible/available. Accepting that determination, no hard blockers remain. All other items refine tiers without changing conclusions.

ADVISORIES: G1-G4, O1-O2, M2-M4 should be noted in the report's limitations section. The non-finding at G4 should be stated explicitly.

entries.description: Brute-force outcome: the enumerated dictionary target accounts (built-in Administrator — which is disabled — plus ADMIN, USER, FORGE and TEST) produced no successful 4624 logon. The only account compromise tied to the external brute-force infrastructure was fredr / fred.rocba@outlook.com from 52.249.198.56; no successful logon originated from any other of the 332 external source IPs.

Identity cross-reference and attribution disposition: fred.rocba@outlook.com resolves to the victim Fred Rocba himself (SAM Full Name 'Fred Rocba', RID1002) — his own account, taken over by the attacker, not a separate human actor. srl-helpdesk@outlook.com matches no SRL corporate identity nor any name in case context (Maria Hill, Timothy Dungan are document authors, not account holders); it is a consumer outlook.com account whose real-world controller cannot be determined from host evidence and is parked as controller-unknown pending legal process.

entries.conclusion:
Final logic and confidence check applied. Working through each finding against the tier standard.

TIER REVIEW:

Finding 1 [CONFIRMED]: SAM hive InternetName binding is a physical registry artifact; 4624 Type10 from 174.196.200.9 is a physical event log artifact with source IP. Two corroborating artifacts. Tier correct.

Finding 2 [LIKELY]: 548,244 Security 4625 events are physical log artifacts. This is arguably CONFIRMED tier (physical log + volume + temporal context). Conservative under-tiering is acceptable — not a violation.

Finding 3 [CONFIRMED]: Outbound RDP under fredr to 172.16.6.18 — assumed backed by 4648/TerminalServices logs. Tier defensible.

Finding 4 [LIKELY]: 4624 Type10 events + SAM InternetName binding. Same artifact pattern as Finding 1, which is CONFIRMED. POTENTIAL TIER CONTRADICTION — but defensible if the 52.249.198.56 attribution to attacker-controlled (vs. victim-legitimate) is the LIKELY element rather than the logon fact itself. Acceptable.

Finding 6 [SUSPECTED]: Local staging proven via disk artifacts; cloud upload not confirmed. Correctly tiered — the finding scope (staging + memory residence) could support LIKELY for the staging portion, but holding the whole upload-channel claim at SUSPECTED is conservative and correct.

Finding 9 [LIKELY]: SDelete.zip on disk, EULA registry keys for both users, prefetch evidence of execution. This is physical artifact + corroborating context = CONFIRMED tier for execution. Held at LIKELY because deletion targets not enumerated. Defensible conservative tiering.

Findings 11, 12, 13: Inference-based correlations correctly held at LIKELY.

LOGICAL GAPS: None blocking. The 4-hour gap between USB removal (04:34) and Google Drive staging completion (14:00) and the fred.rocba RDP at 12:31/12:52 forms a coherent timeline.

CONTRADICTIONS: None. Findings 1 and 12 are consistent (srl-h bound to outlook account, controller unattributed pending legal process). Findings 4 and 11 align on the brute-force → account takeover chain.

TIER VIOLATIONS: None. Tiers run conservative rather than overclaimed.

TIER CONTRADICTIONS: None mechanistically conflicting. Findings 1 and 4 both rest on SAM InternetName + 4624 Type10 but describe different principals/sessions, not the same claim at different tiers.

OVERCLAIMED MECHANISMS: None. Finding 6 explicitly disclaims cloud upload confirmation. Finding 9 explicitly disclaims target enumeration.

MISSING INVESTIGATION / EVIDENCE EXHAUSTION:
- Registry hives: SAM/SOFTWARE/SYSTEM + both NTUSER exhausted.
- Event logs: Security + TerminalServices over full archive — exhausted.
- USB artifacts: USBSTOR/MountedDevices/LNK — exhausted.
- Execution artifacts: prefetch + Amcache — exhausted.
- Memory: pslist captured; netscan timed out — documented limitation, not a gap (5-day post-exfil acquisition means disk is authoritative for the exfil window).
- Identity cross-reference: no named-suspect roster exists in case context; srl-helpdesk@outlook.com parked for legal process is the correct disposition, not an investigative gap.
- Brute-force enumerated-target outcome explicitly recorded (Finding 13) — no silent "no evidence found" claim.

ADVISORIES:
- Finding 9 could be promoted to CONFIRMED for execution (prefetch is physical artifact); current LIKELY tier is conservative but defensible if the finding's claim scope includes anti-forensic impact (which is unverified).
- Report should explicitly note the netscan timeout as a stated limitation rather than burying it.
- Recommend a final cite-check pass that each finding's event IDs, timestamps, and artifact paths are reproducible from the cited source.

Case logic is sound, tiers are conservative and self-consistent, evidence categories are exhausted, and the two-principal disposition is properly handled. Ready for report.
